If customers think your prices are too high, they will leave without buying. A safe starting rule is to set prices at 1.2x to 1.3x the cost you paid."}],["$","p",null,{"className":"text-zinc-300 leading-relaxed","children":"Pay attention to the customer feedback bubbles above their heads. Green smiley faces mean good prices; red angry faces mean you are overcharging. Adjust gradually and find the sweet spot for each product category."}]]}]
8:["$","section",null,{"children":[["$","div",null,{"className":"flex items-center gap-3 mb-4","children":[["$","$L3",null,{"iconNode":[["path",{"d":"M16 21v-2a4 4 0 0 0-4-4H6a4 4 0 0 0-4 4v2","key":"1yyitq"}],["path",{"d":"M16 3.128a4 4 0 0 1 0 7.744","key":"16gr8j"}],["path",{"d":"M22 21v-2a4 4 0 0 0-3-3.87","key":"kshegd"}],["circle",{"cx":"9","cy":"7","r":"4","key":"nufk8"}]],"className":"lucide-users w-6 h-6 text-emerald-400"}],["$","h2",null,{"className":"text-2xl font-bold","children":"When to Hire Your First Employee"}]]}],["$","p",null,{"className":"text-zinc-300 leading-relaxed","children":"Do not rush to hire staff. In the early game, your own labor is free. Once you have at least 6-8 shelves and find yourself constantly running between restocking and checkout, consider hiring a cashier. Cashiers handle the register so you can focus on keeping shelves full. Wait until you have steady daily profits of at least $500 before adding staff."}]]}]
